draft.introduction: 2 Corinthians 1:3–11 opens the epistle with a theology of suffering and consolation, grounding Paul’s authority in his experience of affliction. The God who comforts the afflicted enables them to comfort others in turn.

translation.text: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction, so that we may be able to comfort those who are in any affliction, with the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God.
translation.original_text: εὐλογητὸς ὁ θεὸς καὶ πατὴρ τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ ὁ πατὴρ τῶν οἰκτιρμῶν

translator_notes.note: Paul’s theology of shared suffering (παθήματα) anticipates the Eastern Orthodox understanding that participation in Christ’s suffering is itself a form of theosis—a sharing in the divine life through the pattern of death and resurrection.
